---
title: "Lab 07 — t-tests and ANOVA"
author: "EAES 480 — Modern Statistics in Earth & Environmental Science"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: cosmo
    highlight: tango
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: inline
---

```{r setup, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(janitor)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE
)
```

# Overview

This lab extends hypothesis testing from **claims about one mean** to:

- **one-sample t-tests**
- **two-sample t-tests**
- **paired t-tests**
- **ANOVA**

You will use the **US-AMS simplified AmeriFlux dataset** again and compare means across different time-based groups.

The core logic stays the same:

> estimate → uncertainty → test statistic → p-value

This lab emphasizes both **manual understanding** and **automated testing in R**.

---

# Learning goals

By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

- Compute and interpret a **one-sample t-test**
- Distinguish between **two-sample** and **paired** t-tests
- Compute and interpret **degrees of freedom**
- Explain the structure of an **ANOVA**
- Compare manual calculations to `t.test()` and `aov()`
- Use visualizations to support statistical interpretation
- Produce a fully reproducible R Markdown analysis

---

# Data

## Load and inspect

```{r load_data, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# GOAL: Read the dataset and keep 2022–2023.

df <- read_csv("data/us-ams-simple.csv", na = c("-9999")) %>%
  clean_names() %>%
  filter(year_local %in% c(2022, 2023))

glimpse(df)
```

## Derive time columns if needed

```{r derive_time, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
# GOAL: Create date / month columns if they do not already exist.

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(floor(doy) - 1, origin = paste0(year_local, "-01-01")),
    month = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE)
  )
```

---

# Part I — One-sample t-test

## 1) Choose a day and a variable

Select:
- one **year**
- one **numeric variable**

Examples:
- `ta`
- `swc`
- `le`
- `fc`

```{r choose_one_sample, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
target_year <- 2023
target_var  <- "swc"

sample_data <- df %>%
  filter(year_local == target_year) %>% 
  slice_sample(n = 200)

x <- sample_data[[target_var]]

mean(x, na.rm = TRUE); sd(x, na.rm = TRUE); length(x)
```

> What variable and day did you choose, and why?

> I chose the variable `swc`, soil water content because it carries important implications about precipitation and climate changes as soil dries out with warming. I chose 200 samples to get an ample amount of data.

---

## 2) State a null hypothesis

Write a claim about the mean of your chosen variable on that day.

Examples:
- “The mean temperature on this day is 15 °C.”
- “The mean latent heat flux on this day is 100 W m$^{-2}$.”

Fill in:

> $H_0$: `The mean soil water content on this day is 21%.`

> $H_A$: `The mean soil water content on this day is not 21%.`

---

## 3) Compute the one-sample t-statistic manually

Use:

$t = (\bar{x} - \mu_0) / (s / \sqrt{n})$

```{r one_sample_manual, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
mu0 <- 21

xbar <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
n <- sum(!is.na(x))

t_stat <- (xbar - mu0) / (s / sqrt(n))
t_stat
```

---

## 4) Compute the p-value and degrees of freedom

```{r one_sample_pvalue, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
df_t <- n - 1

p_value <- 2 * pt(-abs(t_stat), df = df_t)

tibble(
  xbar = xbar,
  s = s,
  n = n,
  df_t = df_t,
  t_stat = t_stat,
  p_value = p_value
)
```

> What does the p-value suggest about your null hypothesis?

> Since the p-value is very small, and certainly smaller than the threshold of 0.05, it is statistically significant and we can reject the null hypothesis. 

---

## 5) Compare with `t.test()`

```{r one_sample_ttest, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
t.test(x, mu = mu0)
```

> Does the `t.test()` output agree with your manual calculation? If not, why might that be?

> The t-test output does agree, as the p-value is still much smaller than 0.05.

---

# Part II — Two-sample t-test

## 6) Compare the same variable across two day ranges

Choose:
- the **same variable**
- two different **day ranges in 2023**

```{r choose_two_sample, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
x1 <- df %>%
  filter(year_local == 2023, doy > 50 & doy < 60) %>%
  pull(target_var)

x2 <- df %>%
  filter(year_local == 2023, doy > 100 & doy < 110) %>%
  pull(target_var)

mean(x1, na.rm = TRUE); sd(x1, na.rm = TRUE); length(x1)
mean(x2, na.rm = TRUE); sd(x2, na.rm = TRUE); length(x2)
```

> Why did you choose these two days?

> I chose these two days because they're at very different times of the year, so there is a more rounded sense of the data. They also represent different seasons during the year.

---

## 7) Summarize the difference in means

```{r two_sample_summary, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
xbar1 <- mean(x1, na.rm = TRUE)
xbar2 <- mean(x2, na.rm = TRUE)

s1 <- sd(x1, na.rm = TRUE)
s2 <- sd(x2, na.rm = TRUE)

n1 <- sum(!is.na(x1))
n2 <- sum(!is.na(x2))

mean_diff <- xbar1 - xbar2

tibble(
  xbar1 = xbar1,
  xbar2 = xbar2,
  mean_diff = mean_diff
)
```

---

## 8) Compute the two-sample t-statistic manually (Welch)

```{r two_sample_manual, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
se_diff <- sqrt((s1^2 / n1) + (s2^2 / n2))

t_stat <- mean_diff / se_diff

df_welch <- ((s1^2 / n1 + s2^2 / n2)^2) /
  (((s1^2 / n1)^2 / (n1 - 1)) + ((s2^2 / n2)^2 / (n2 - 1)))

p_value <- 2 * pt(-abs(t_stat), df = df_welch)

tibble(
  mean_diff = mean_diff,
  se_diff = se_diff,
  t_stat = t_stat,
  df_welch = df_welch,
  p_value = p_value
)
```

> What does the sign of `mean_diff` mean? What does the p-value mean?

> The sign of `mean-diff`, in this case being positive, means that the mean of the second group, around day 100, is higher than that of the mean from day 50. The p-value being very small and much smaller than 0.05 means that we will reject the null hypothesis because the mean soil water content from these two days is not 21%.

---

## 9) Compare with `t.test()`

```{r two_sample_ttest, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
t.test(x1, x2)
```

> Does the automated t-test agree with your manual result?

> The t-test does agree with my manual result.

---

## 10) Visualize the two groups

```{r two_sample_plot, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}

day_range1 <- "50-60"
day_range2 <- "100-110"

plot_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(group = paste0("DOY_", day_range1), value = x1),
  tibble(group = paste0("DOY_", day_range2), value = x2)
)

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = group, y = value, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = target_var,
    title = "Two-sample comparison"
  )
```

> Does the plot match your statistical conclusion?

> I would say the plot does match my statistical conclusion since there is hardly any overlap between the two days, meaning they do not share many values regarding mean soil water content and would have significantly different mean values. The range of values from day 100-110 is also much greater. 

---

# Part III — Paired t-test

## 11) Compare the same days across two years

Choose one DOY range and compare **2022 vs 2023** for the same variable.

```{r paired_setup, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
x1 <- df %>%
  filter(year_local == 2022, doy > 175 & doy < 200) %>%
  arrange(date) %>%
  pull(target_var)

x2 <- df %>%
  filter(year_local == 2023, doy > 175 & doy < 200) %>%
  arrange(date) %>%
  pull(target_var)

length(x1); length(x2)
```

> Why is this a paired comparison rather than an independent two-sample comparison?

> This is a paired comparison rather than independent two-sample because the days across the two years are paired together. This is because they are the same day, just a year apart, so they are not considered independent samples. 

---

## 12) Compute paired differences manually

```{r paired_manual, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE}
d <- x1 - x2

dbar <- mean(d, na.rm = TRUE)
sd_d <- sd(d, na.rm = TRUE)
n <- sum(!is.na(d))

t_stat <- dbar / (sd_d / sqrt(n))
p_value <- 2 * pt(-abs(t_stat), df = n - 1)

tibble(
  dbar = dbar,
  sd_d = sd_d,
  n = n,
  t_stat = t_stat,
  p_value = p_value
)
```

> What does the sign of `dbar` mean?

> The negative sign of `dbar` means that the x2 value is larger than that of x1.

---

## 13) Compare with `t.test(..., paired = TRUE)`

```{r paired_ttest, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE}
t.test(x1, x2, paired = TRUE)
```

> How does the paired t-test differ conceptually from the two-sample t-test?

> The paired t-test differs conceptually since the observations are related to each other, or the same subject twice, rather than independent sets of observations that are not as related. There is more variability in a two-sample t-test. 

---

# Part IV — ANOVA

## 14) Create a categorical grouping variable

Group `ta` into broad seasonal categories:

- Summer
- Winter
- Other

```{r make_groups_anova, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
df_anova <- df %>%
  filter(!is.na(ta)) %>%
  mutate(
    month_group = case_when(
      month %in% c("Jun", "Jul", "Aug") ~ "Summer",
      month %in% c("Nov", "Dec", "Jan") ~ "Winter",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    )
  )

df_anova %>% count(month_group)
```

---

## 15) Visualize the groups

```{r plot_anova_groups, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
ggplot(df_anova, aes(x = month_group, y = ta, fill = month_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "ta",
    title = "Air temperature by seasonal group"
  )
```

> Do the groups look different enough that ANOVA might detect a difference?

> The groups do look different enough. There is a lot of space between the datasets, or little overlap between their points and especially between the means. 

---

## 16) Compute ANOVA components manually

```{r anova_manual_components, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
grand_mean <- mean(df_anova$ta, na.rm = TRUE)

group_stats <- df_anova %>%
  group_by(month_group) %>%
  summarise(
    group_mean = mean(ta, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ss_between <- sum(group_stats$n * (group_stats$group_mean - grand_mean)^2)

df_anova2 <- df_anova %>%
  left_join(group_stats, by = "month_group")

ss_within <- sum((df_anova2$ta - df_anova2$group_mean)^2)

k <- n_distinct(df_anova$month_group)
n_total <- nrow(df_anova)

df_between <- k - 1
df_within <- n_total - k

ms_between <- ss_between / df_between
ms_within <- ss_within / df_within

f_stat <- ms_between / ms_within
p_value <- pf(f_stat, df1 = df_between, df2 = df_within, lower.tail = FALSE)

tibble(
  grand_mean = grand_mean,
  ss_between = ss_between,
  ss_within = ss_within,
  df_between = df_between,
  df_within = df_within,
  f_stat = f_stat,
  p_value = p_value
)
```

> In your own words, what do `ss_between` and `ss_within` represent?

> I would say that `ss_between` represents the difference between the groups and their means. `ss_within` represents moreso the difference and variability within a group itself. 

---

## 17) Compare with `aov()`

```{r run_anova, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
model <- aov(ta ~ month_group, data = df_anova)
summary(model)
```

> What does the ANOVA p-value tell you? Does it tell you which groups differ?

> The ANOVA p-value, at least with these variables, tells me that there is variability amongst the mean values. However, it does not tell me which group or groups differ in these values. 

---

## 18) Final reflection

Answer in **5–7 sentences**:

- How is a one-sample t-test different from a two-sample t-test?
- Why is a paired t-test special?
- How is ANOVA a generalization of the t-test?
- What stayed the same across all these tests?

> A one-sample t-test is different from a two-sample one because the former compares the means to a known value, while the latter compares the means of two independent groups. A paired t-test is then special since it is comparing two dependent sets of observations rather than independent ones. ANOVA is a generalization of the t-test since it allows the comparison of more than two groups. In this case, three groups were compared. Something that stayed the same across all of the tests used was how each of them was being used to compare differences and variability in mean values. 

---

# Submission

- Knit your `.Rmd` to HTML.
- Commit and push both the `.Rmd` and knitted `.html` to your GitHub repo.
- Submit the GitHub repository link on Canvas.
